q: A plant disease is usually defined as abnormal growth and/or ___ of a plant.
___ diseases are caused by living organisms (e.g., fungi, bacteria, and viruses).
___ diseases are caused by non-living environmental conditions.
Plant disease is any abnormal condition that alters the appearance or ___ of a plant.
Most plant diseases, around 85 percent are caused by ___ or fungal-like organisms.
___ are very tiny roundworms that are plant parasites.
Some plant diseases are classified as "___," or diseases that are non-infectious.
An ___ is capable of reproducing within or on its host and spreading from one susceptible host to another.
The study of plant diseases is called ___.
Pathology is derived from the two Greek words ___ (suffering, disease) and logos (discourse, study).
___ diseases can spread throughout one plant and also may spread to neighbouring plants of the same species.
Biotic diseases sometimes show physical evidence (___) of the pathogen.
Abiotic diseases are caused by nonliving factors, such as ___ , sunscald, freeze injury, wind injury, chemical injury, nutrient deficiency, or improper cultural practices.
Abiotic diseases are caused by conditions ___ to the plant, not due to living agents.
___ damage often occurs on many plant species.
The fungi that cause tomato leaf blight do not cause damage on ___ .
Plant diseases their occurrence and severity result from the impact of three factors: the host plant, the pathogen and the ___ .
___ of plant disease are physical evidence of the pathogen.
___ are the actual organisms causing the disease.
Signs include conks, fruiting bodies, mildew, mushrooms, mycelium, ___ , slime flux or ooze, spore masses, insects and/or their frass.
___ are visible effects of disease on plants.
Any detectable changes in colour, shape, and/or functions of the plant in response to a pathogen or disease-causing agent is a ___ .

Plant symptoms include blight, canker, ___ , decline, dieback, distortion, gall or gall-like, gummosis, leaf distortion, leaf scorch, leaf spot, mosaic, necrosis, stunting, wilt, witches' broom, insect feeding injury.
___ cause mottling, spots, mosaic-like patterns, crinkling, and other malformations on leaves and fruits.
Because viruses are ___ , infected plants must be rogued or discarded.
Common virus diseases in Colorado include ___ of tomatoes.
Infectious plant diseases are caused by ___ that attack and obtain their nutrition from the plant they infect.
Fungi and ___ cause more plant diseases than any other group of plant pest.
FLOs are organisms like ___ and Phytophthora and cause downy mildew.
Most pathogenic fungi belong to the genera: Alternaria, Aspergillus, Botrytis, Fusarium, ___ , Rhizopus, and Sclerotinia.
The genus ___ causes leaf spots and blight of various plants.
The genus ___ causes rots of seeds.
The genus ___ causes blights of various plants.
The genus ___ causes root rot of many plants.
The genus ___ causes rust of cereals.
The genus ___ causes soft rot of fruits.
The genus ___ causes soft rot of vegetables.
Bacteria that cause plant diseases are most active in ___ and humid environments.
Most plant pathogenic bacteria belong to the genera: Agrobacterium, Erwinia, Pseudomonas, ___ and Xanthomonas.
The genus ___ causes crown gall of various plants.
The genus ___ causes fire blight of pears and apple, wilt of cucurbits and corn, soft rot of fruit.
The genus ___ causes leaf spot of tobacco, blight of peas and soybeans.
The genus ___ causes scab of potato, pox of sweet potato.
The genus ___ causes blight of beans, rice and walnut, leaf spot of fruits, black rot of crucifers, citrus canker.
Viruses are ___ pathogenic particles that infect other living organisms.
One of the most common vectors of viruses are ___ .
___ are characterized by the formation of light green, yellow, or white spots intermingled with the normal green aerial plant structures.
___ are characterized by the appearance of chlorotic or necrotic rings on the leaves.
Nematodes are simple, multicellular animals and are ___ , soft-bodied, non-segmented roundworms.
Plant parasitic nematodes have a ___ , a piercing mouthpart.
Based on the plant part, the nematode invaded can be classified into wheat nematode: ___ .
Biological control is control of plant diseases using ___ .
___ is the secretion of metabolites harmful to the pathogens.
Antagonist may compete with the pathogens for ___ or space.
___ methods of disease prevention and control are based on the physiological tolerance of disease agents to adverse conditions.
Chemicals used to control bacteria are called ___ .
Chemicals used to control fungi are called ___ .
Chemicals used to control nematodes are called ___ .
A disease is an illness or disorder of the body or mind that leads to ___ .
___ diseases are those that are not caused by pathogens, so that they cannot spread from one person to another.
Most cardiovascular disease starts with ___ formation.
If the ___ , the inner lining of the wall of the artery, is damaged by high blood pressure, an inflammatory response follows.
White blood cells (mostly ___ ) move into the area to form an atheroma.
The hardening of arteries, caused by atheroma, is called ___ .
In ___ , plaques build up slowly in the coronary arteries, reducing blood flow to the parts of the heart muscle beyond the plaques.
In angina, the narrowed coronary arteries cannot supply enough oxygenated blood and the heart muscle resorts to ___ .
Angina causes a ___ in the chest that can extend into the arms, particularly the left one, and the jaw.
___ occurs when a coronary artery becomes completely blocked by a blood clot.
Myocardial infarction is more commonly known as a ___ .
A ___ is caused by an interruption to the normal blood supply to an area of the brain.
___ is the formation of a blood clot in a vein deep inside the body.
DVT usually happens in ___ .
The three main risk factors for CVDs which cannot be changed are Genes, Age and ___ .
___ are more likely to suffer from heart disease than women.
Substances in ___ can damage the artery linings, which makes the build-up of plaques more likely.
___ can lead to heart diseases and also causes obesity, high blood pressure and diabetes.
A healthy blood pressure is around $120 mmHg$ during ___ .
A healthy blood pressure is around $80 mmHg$ during ___ .
Blood pressure regularly above $140/90 mmHg$ is a sign of ___ .
A body mass index (BMI) of $18.5-25 kgm^{-2}$ is the ___ .
___ drugs such as beta blockers, sympathetic nerve inhibitors, ACE inhibitors are commonly prescribed to reduce blood pressure.
To lower the level of cholesterol in the blood, a group of drugs known as ___ is used.
Statins improve the balance of ___ to HDLs.
___ compounds are now widely sold in spreads and yoghurts to reduce the levels of LDLs in the blood.
___ (e.g., warfarin and heparin) are a class of medicines used to reduce blood clotting.
___ drugs such as aspirin are also a type of anticoagulant.
___ diseases are diseases that are caused by organisms known as pathogens.
The way in which a pathogen passes from one host to another is called the ___ .
The ___ of a disease is the number of people who have that disease at any one time.
Diseases that are always in populations are described as ___ .
An ___ occurs when there is a sudden increase in the number of people with a disease.
A ___ occurs when there is an increase in the number of cases throughout a continent or across the world.
The death rate from different diseases is referred to as ___ .
___ is caused by the bacterium Vibrio cholerae.
___ is caused by the bacterium Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
Malaria is caused by four species of the protist ___ .
The insect vector for malaria is the ___ .
Site of action of the malaria pathogen includes liver, ___ , and brain.
Female Anopheles mosquitoes feed on human blood to obtain the ___ they need to develop their eggs.
Male and female gametes of Plasmodium fuse in the ___ .
The infective stages pass from the mosquito's ___ into the human's blood.
Plasmodium can also pass across the ___ from mother to fetus.
Quinine and ___ are anti-malarial drugs used to treat infected people.
Chloroquine inhibits ___ and prevents the parasite spreading within the body.
___ has the added advantage of inhibiting the sexual reproduction of Plasmodium inside the biting mosquito.
___ is a drug derived from the plant compound artemisinin.
The antibiotic ___ is also used as a prophylactic drug for malaria.
Stocking ponds with fish such as ___ is a biological control measure for malaria.
Spraying a preparation containing the bacterium ___ kills mosquito larvae.
Mosquitoes became resistant to ___ and other insecticides such as dieldrin.
